title: How Data Flows Through Your Application
subtitle: Controller → Service → Repository → Database — the layered architecture

sections.title: The Layered Architecture
sections.text:
Real applications organize code into layers. Think of it like a restaurant:

  • Controller — The waiter. Takes your order, brings your food. Handles HTTP stuff but never touches the kitchen.
  • Service — The chef. Decides what to cook, applies the rules. "This order needs gluten-free bread."
  • Repository — The pantry. Knows where ingredients are stored. Talks to the database so nobody else has to.

This separation keeps your code organized. You can change the database without touching the chef's recipes. You can change the menu without touching the pantry. Each layer has one job.
sections.caption: Request flows through Controller → Service → Repository → Database and back
sections.callout: Keep each layer focused on one job. A controller that queries the database directly is a maintainability nightmare. Layer separation is worth the extra files.

sections.title: The Food Delivery Example
sections.text:
Let's trace a real order through the layers. A user places an order in a food delivery app:

  1. Controller receives POST /orders with the cart. It checks the JSON is valid and passes the data to the service.
  2. Service calculates the total, checks stock, applies discounts, verifies the payment. This is where the real decisions happen.
  3. Repository saves the order to the database, creates item records. It just stores things.
  4. The response flows back: data → formatted → sent as 201 Created.

Each layer is replaceable. Want to switch from PostgreSQL to MongoDB? Only the repository changes. Add audit logging? Just the service. This is how professional codebases work.
sections.caption: An order submission flows through all three layers before returning the response
sections.detail: This layered approach shines as your application grows. When you need to add audit logging, you add it in the service layer — not scattered across controllers. When you migrate from PostgreSQL to MongoDB, you only change the repository layer. The controller and service never know the database changed. This is the power of dependency inversion and separation of concerns.
